coredata.dc:title: Carbonized polymer dots activated hierarchical tungsten oxide for efficient and stable triethylamine sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Mingxin
Zhao, Zhihui
Hui, Bin
Sun, Jianhua
Sun, Jianxin
Tian, Weiliang
Zhang, Ziqi
Zhang, Kewei
Xia, Yanzhi
coredata.dc:description: Hierarchical metal oxide semiconductors present great potential in detecting toxic and hazardous gases with special emphasis on the regulation of their structures and compositions to advance sensor performance. Herein, marine polysaccharide derived carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) are presented to activate hierarchical tungsten oxide (WO3) as efficient and stable triethylamine sensor. Owing to the promoted receptor and transducer function of the oxide/polymer/carbon heterostructure, the CPDs/WO3 sensor exhibits extraordinary sensing characteristics for triethylamine detection, including higher response (4.3 times), faster response/recovery (4.3 times/2.1 times), lower operating temperature (30 °C) and lower detection limit (2.4 times) as compared with hierarchical WO3 sensor, which are also superior to most of the previous reports related to triethylamine detection. Importantly, the adsorption-desorption kinetic of WO3 is found to be enhanced by 67 times after introducing CPDs, mainly derived from abundant slit-like channels for gas diffuse, desirable defect feature as reactive sites, and favorable 0D–2D interface for charge transfer and transport. This work not only establishes an alternative strategy for promoting metal oxide semiconductor gas sensors but also provides a fundamental understanding of CPDs in gas-sensing field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Triethylamine
Gas sensor
Tungsten oxide
Carbonized polymer dots
Marine polysaccharide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and gas-sensing test of the sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51973099 , 21761029 ), the Taishan Scholar Program of Shandong Province (Nos. tsqn201812055 , tspd20181208 ), the State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University) (Nos. ZKT04 , GZRC202007 ), the Dean Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology (No. 2017K005 ), and the Engineering Laboratory of Chemical Resources Utilization in South Xinjiang of Xinjiang Production and Construction Corps ( CRUZD2003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
(a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of CPDs/WO3·H2O architecture. (b) TEM image of as-prepared CPDs. Inset shows the corresponding statistical analysis of the size distribution. (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum (λ
ex
=365 nm) of as-prepared CPDs. Inset shows photographs of the CPDs solution under daylight and UV light. (d,e) SEM images of WO3·H2O (d) and CPDs/WO3·H2O (e). (f) Elemental mapping of as-prepared CPDs/WO3·H2O, showing W, O and C distributions.

(a) XRD patterns of as-prepared CPDs/WO3·H2O, annealed WO3 and CPDs/WO3. (b) FT-IR spectra of the CPDs, WO3 and CPDs/WO3. (c) Raman spectra of the WO3 and CPDs/WO3. High-resolution XPS spectra, showing (d-f) W4f of the WO3 and CPDs/WO3 (d), O1s of the CPDs/WO3 (e), and C1s of the CPDs/WO3 (f).

(a) Schematic illustration of the fabrication procedure of gas sensor. (b) Response of the sensors to 40 ppm triethylamine under different operating temperatures. (c,d) Transient plots (c) and corresponding response value (d), response time and recovery time as a function of CPDs amounts. (e) Transient plots of the sensors at optimal operating temperature with marked response and recovery time. (f) Response of the sensors to various 40 ppm target gases at 140 °C. (g) Five-axe spider-web diagram for evaluating sensor parameter of the WO3 (orange color) and CPDs/WO3 (blue color). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Transient plots of the WO3 and CPDs/WO3 sensors to ppm-level triethylamine. (b) Relationships between the sensor responses and triethylamine concentrations. (c) Experimental and fitted transient responses of the CPDs/WO3 sensor under various triethylamine concentrations. (d) Variation of transient response, surface rate constant and response time of the CPDs/WO3 sensor with triethylamine concentration.
Adsorption-desorption kinetic during the gas-sensing process of (a) the WO3 sensor, and (b) the CPDs/WO3 sensor.

(a) Response of the CPDs/WO3 sensor to 40 ppm triethylamine under different relative humidity. (b) Long-term stability of the CPDs/WO3 sensor to 40 ppm triethylamine. (c,d) Mixed gas detection of ethanol, isopropanol and triethylamine by (c) the WO3 sensor and (d) the CPDs/WO3 sensor. Mixed gas I: ethanol: isopropanol: triethylamine= 10 ppm: 10 ppm: 10 ppm; Mixed gas II: ethanol: isopropanol: triethylamine= 20 ppm: 20 ppm: 20 ppm; Mixed gas III: ethanol: isopropanol: triethylamine= 40 ppm: 40 ppm: 40 ppm.

(a) UV–vis absorbance spectra and estimated band gap values of WO3 and CPDs/WO3. (b–d) Deconvoluted PL spectra of CPDs (b), WO3 (c) and CPDs/WO3 (d), showing defect states of corresponding gas-sensing materials. (e) Schematic energy-band diagram of the CPDs/WO3 sensor and corresponding gas-sensing mechanism upon exposure to triethylamine.
Comparison of triethylamine response of various metal oxide semiconductor gas sensors.

head.ce:title: Carbonized polymer dots activated hierarchical tungsten oxide for efficient and stable triethylamine sensor

R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology, Guangxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering in South Xinjiang, College of Life Science, Tarim University, Alar 843300, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering in South Xinjiang, College of Life Science, Tarim University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering in South Xinjiang, College of Life Science, Tarim University, Alar 843300, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hierarchical metal oxide semiconductors present great potential in detecting toxic and hazardous gases with special emphasis on the regulation of their structures and compositions to advance sensor performance. Herein, marine polysaccharide derived carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) are presented to activate hierarchical tungsten oxide (WO3) as efficient and stable triethylamine sensor. Owing to the promoted receptor and transducer function of the oxide/polymer/carbon heterostructure, the CPDs/WO3 sensor exhibits extraordinary sensing characteristics for triethylamine detection, including higher response (4.3 times), faster response/recovery (4.3 times/2.1 times), lower operating temperature (30 °C) and lower detection limit (2.4 times) as compared with hierarchical WO3 sensor, which are also superior to most of the previous reports related to triethylamine detection. Importantly, the adsorption-desorption kinetic of WO3 is found to be enhanced by 67 times after introducing CPDs, mainly derived from abundant slit-like channels for gas diffuse, desirable defect feature as reactive sites, and favorable 0D–2D interface for charge transfer and transport. This work not only establishes an alternative strategy for promoting metal oxide semiconductor gas sensors but also provides a fundamental understanding of CPDs in gas-sensing field.

ce:section.ce:para: With rapid development of modern industry, the accurate detection of toxic and hazardous gases is of special necessity for environmental safety and human health (Giannoukos et al., 2016; Jian et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2021); for example, when the concentration of triethylamine in air is above the threshold limit of 10 ppm, the inhalation or skin contact of gas leads to serious tissue damage, especially to eyes and respiratory tract (Bai et al., 2020; Gui et al., 2019a; Hu et al., 2020). It is essential to develop efficient and stable gas sensor for detecting ppm-level triethylamine with high sensitivity and good selectivity. Among various types of gas sensors, metal oxide semiconductor sensor is the most economical, convenient, and efficient (Van den Broek et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2020a, 2020b), which working based on the resistance changes caused by gas adsorption or reaction in the active sensing layer. Therefore, it is highly desirable to advance material structures and compositions to achieve improved sensing performance (Ma et al., 2018). In this respect, a variety of hierarchical metal oxides (such as WO3 (Bai et al., 2014c), ZnO (Kim et al., 2017), Fe2O3 (Teng et al., 2020) and In2O3 (Zhou et al., 2020)) and their heterostructures (such as WO3/NiO (Wei et al., 2020b), SnO2/Sn3O4 (Zeng et al., 2019), Fe2O3/NiO (Bai et al., 2020), and ZnO/MoS2 (Chang et al., 2020) and NiO/BiVO4 (Bai et al., 2021)) have recently been constructed to realize better sensor performance, owing to their high surface-to-volume ratios and well-defined mesoporous channels. Although great progress has been achieved, the present metal oxide semiconductor sensor still suffers from the drawbacks of baseline drift, high-power consumption and limited response and selectivity (Kumar et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2016). It is thus necessary to exploit novel gas-sensitive materials for satisfying the requirement of low-temperature gas detection.

Carbonaceous nanomaterials, which generally include carbon nanotube, graphene, graphene oxide and carbon dots, have attracted increasing attention and been widely used to fabricate multifarious sensors due to their excellent chemical and electrical properties (Li et al., 2019; Travlou et al., 2015). Compared with the mentioned carbonaceous materials, carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) as a novel promising functional material possess some unique properties related to surface polar groups, making it easily composite with other semiconductor materials without phase separation (Xia et al., 2019). Recently, enhanced charge-transfer ability has been observed in CPD/TiO2 photonic crystals (Zhao et al., 2019) and CPD-modified perovskite solar cells (Zhao et al., 2020), which is predicted to be pivotal for the promotion of metal oxide semiconductor sensors. The strong coupling between CPDs and metal oxides would enable effective gas adsorption and efficient charge transfer, which would lead to high gas-sensing performance of the heterostructure. Particularly, compared with some traditional quantum dots containing cadmium or lead, the CPDs possess low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and facile preparation (Liu et al., 2020). Varieties of small molecules, polymers, and even biomass such as kinds of seaweeds can be chosen as low-cost sources to produce CPDs. So far, the study of CPDs in gas sensors has not been mentioned in literature, and there is a lack of conclusive data establishing.

In this work, we present a facile strategy for activating hierarchical WO3 with CPDs as desirable gas sensor. The CPDs/WO3 sensing layer with oxide/polymer/carbon heterostructure has been successfully prepared from marine polysaccharides and tungstic acid through a hydrothermal-ultrasonic combination procedure, and exhibits improved sensing characteristics for triethylamine detection, including high response, low operating temperature, short response/recovery time and small detection limit. The performance improvement is attributed to the promoted receptor function and transducer function, mainly derived from the abundant slit-like pores for gas diffuse, desirable defect feature as reactive sites, favorable 0D–2D interface for charge transfer and transport. This work not only presents a novel strategy for sensitizing metal oxide semiconductor gas sensors but also looks into the fundamental understanding of carbonized polymer dots in gas-sensing field.

The CPDs were prepared through hydrothermal treatment of the chemically crosslinked sodium alginate (SA) via glutaraldehyde, as described previously (Sun et al., 2020). Typically, 1.000 g of SA powder was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water. After continuous stirring at room temperature, 0.8 mL of glutaraldehyde and 1.0 mL of HCl solution (0.1 M) were added dropwise into the above solution. Then, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave to react at 180 °C for 8 h. After reaction, the obtained brown solution was filtered with nylon filter membrane to remove the carbonized precipitate, and then dialyzed with deionized water in a dialysis bag (1000 D). Finally, the concentration of obtained CPDs solution was determined to be 13 mgmL−1.

In a typical procedure, 3.912 g of Na2WO4·2H2O and 1.500 g of oxalic acid were dissolved in 48 mL of deionized water. After 20 min of stirring, several milliliters of 3 M HCl solution were added into the above solution drop by drop. Then, a certain amount of as-prepared CPDs solution (1–5 mL) was added, and the reaction system was sonicated for 1 h. Finally, the resulting yellow precipitate was collected by centrifugation and washed several times with distilled water. For comparison, pure WO3 was prepared without the addition of CPDs by a similar procedure.

The product size and surface morphology were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi H-7600) and field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta 250 FEG) equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS) spectrometer. The crystallographic structure was determined using X-ray diffraction (XRD, DX2700) at 40 K and 30 mA. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with Nicolet iso50 instrument (Thermo Fisher). Raman spectra was obtained on HR800 Raman spectroscope (Horiba). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were taken by using Escalab 250Xi+ spectrometer (Thermo Fisher). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra were observed on U-3900H spectrophotometer (Hitachi). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were carried out on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and gas-sensing test of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sample was mixed with terpineol and ground in an agate mortar to obtain a uniform paste which was then coated slowly on an Ag/Cr interdigital electrode, followed by spinning at 2000 rpm for 15 s. The above process was repeated several times to form a complete coating. After drying, it was calcined at 400 °C for 2 h to form a successive thin coating layer on the surface of the interdigital electrode. To improve stability, the sensor element was aged overnight at 200 °C. The sensor performance was measured using a home-built computer controlling system including a vacuum chamber that can be heated and a Keithley 2612 data acquisition system. The response of the gas sensor was deduced as S= R0/Rg, where R0 and Rg is the resistance of the sensor in air and target gas, respectively. The response time is defined as the time required for the change in response to reach 90% of the equilibrium value after injecting the gas. The recovery time is defined as the time needed for the sensor to return to 90% of the original response in air after removing the gas.

As illustrated in Fig. 1a, the CPDs/WO3·H2O was prepared through a combination of hydrothermal treatment and ultrasonic procedure. During the hydrothermal process, the dehydration and carbonization of marine polysaccharide (chemically cross-linked sodium alginate) under high temperature and pressure results in compact CPDs with a polymer/carbon hybrid structure (Sun et al., 2020). It is found that the prepared CPDs exhibits an irregular dot shape, as seen from the TEM image in Fig. 1b. The corresponding particle size distribution presents that the diameter of CPDs particles varies from 0.6 to 5.9 nm. There are abundant functional groups (carboxy, hydroxy and carboxyl) on the CPDs surface, which are beneficial for the interaction between sensing layer and analyte gases through covalent or hydrogen bonds (Zhang et al., 2019). Fig. 1c shows light absorbance properties of the CPDs, which has a distinctly continuous and broad absorption under the UV and visible regions. The weak peak at about 310 nm is ascribed to the characteristic n-π* transition of C˭O (Sun et al., 2020). Under UV excitation at 365 nm, the obtained CPDs delivery a strong emission peak around 451 nm, and the aqueous solution exhibits a clear light-blue color (Insert of Fig. 1b). During the ultrasonic process, the ultrasonic cavitation and Ostwald ripening results in WO3·H2O and CPDs/WO3·H2O architectures with nanosheet-assembled structures (Bai et al., 2020, 2014c). Fig. 1d and e shows typical SEM images of the WO3·H2O and CPDs/WO3·H2O, respectively. The surface of the as-prepared microspheres is composed of abundant interlaced nanosheets, which may form slit-like pores beneficial for gas sensors. After the addition of CPDs, the overall morphology of the WO3·H2O almost unchanged. The obtained CPDs/WO3·H2O samples contain large amount of mesopores with a peak BJH pore size of about 3.6 nm (Fig. S1), and the specific surface area was measured to be 97.8 m2 g−1. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) characterization of CPDs/WO3·H2O exhibited the relatively uniform dispersion of W, O and C elements on the whole microsphere surface (Fig. 1f).

The structure and composition of obtained materials were identified by XRD, FT-IR, Raman, and XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. 2a, the as-prepared CPDs/WO3·H2O displays diffraction peaks of orthorhombic WO3·H2O (JCPDS # 43-0679), where the tungsten atom is surrounded by four oxygen atoms, a shorter terminal W˭O bond and a longer W–OH2 bond. After annealing at 400 °C, the CPDs/WO3 displays diffraction peaks of monoclinic WO3 (JCPDS # 43-1035). No peaks of additional phases are detected and the observed diffraction peaks become stronger, indicating the crystallinity of monoclinic WO3 becomes better during the annealing process. The CPDs/WO3 shares a similar XRD pattern of the WO3, suggesting the presence of CPDs has negligible effect on the growth of WO3 architecture. However, a close look at the peaks of CPDs/WO3 reveals that the peak intensities slightly decreased with addition of the CPDs, and even the (002) peak disappears, which may be attributed to the blocked X-ray irradiation and weakened coherent scattering by deposition of CPDs with low content and fine particle size.

For pure WO3 sample (Fig. 2b), the characteristic peaks in the FT-IR spectrum positioned at lower than 1000 cm−1 are caused by the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of W˭O and W-O bonds (Bai et al., 2014b). The O-H bonds at around 3400 cm−1 that appeared in both the CPDs and CPDs/WO3 samples are attributed to the vibration of surface-adsorbed water. The absorption band at 1621 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of CPDs/WO3 is associated with the stretching vibration of C˭O (Gao et al., 2017), suggesting the existence of the polysaccharide-derived CPDs. It is worth noting that the peak located at 1028 cm−1 in the CPDs due to the C-O stretching vibration shifts to 961 cm−1 in the CPDs/WO3, confirming the strong interaction between CPDs and WO3 in the CPDs/WO3 architecture. The interaction between C-O and W-O causes the observed distinct peak shift. The Raman spectrum of WO3 shows well-defined bands at 184, 260, 312, 703 and 801 cm−1 (Fig. 2c), which is in accordance with characteristic modes of monoclinic WO3 (Santato and Odziemkowski, 2001). As compared with WO3, two additional peaks are observed at 1361 and 1580 cm−1 in the Raman spectrum of CPDs/WO3, which corresponds to the D-band carbon (sp3) and G-band carbon (sp2), respectively (Yu et al., 2014). This indicates the successful combination of CPDs and WO3 in the CPDs/WO3 architecture.

To further explore the composition and chemical states, the obtained samples were subjected to XPS study. As shown in Fig. 2d, the spin orbit splitting of the W 4f7/2, W 4f5/2 components exists at around 35.2 eV and 37.3 eV, respectively. As compared to the WO3 sample, the position of the W 4f peak in the CPDs/WO3 slightly shifts to the lower binding energy, suggesting the presence of interaction between the CPDs and WO3. Fig. 2e depicts high-resolution XPS spectrum of the O1s core level region for the CPDs/WO3. After deconvolution, three peaks centered at 530.2, 530.9 and 532.3 eV are observed, which is ascribed to the W-O, C˭O and O-H bond, respectively (Zhang et al., 2017). In the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2f), four fitted sub-peaks at 284.1, 285.3, 287.5 and 289.6 eV are ascribed to the C-C, C-O, C˭O, O-C˭C, respectively, demonstrating the main function groups on the CPDs surface. This is in consistent with the results from above FT-IR and Raman analyses, further confirming the successful preparation of CPDs/WO3 architecture with efficient bonding connection.

To fabricate gas sensors, the samples were deposited onto interdigitated electrodes (IDEs) with 0.15 mm gap through blade-coating technique, followed by thermal calcination, as illustrated in Fig. 3a. Compared with pure WO3, the CPDs/WO3 sensor exhibits a much higher response to triethylamine at a lower operating temperature, demonstrating the advantage of CPDs in improving the gas-sensing behavior of semiconductor metal oxides (Fig. 3b). The response value of the CPDs/WO3 sensor is about 4.3 times higher than that of the hierarchical WO3 sensor, and the optimal operating temperature is determined to be 170 °C and 140 °C for WO3 and CPDs/WO3, respectively. The lowered operating temperature indicates less thermal energy is essential to overcome the activation energy barrier for completing molecular adsorption reaction on the surface of CPDs/WO3. To explore the difference of the CPDs, WO3 and CPDs/WO3 sensors, the R0 change of all sensors with sensor operating temperature were drawn in Fig. S2. The result indicates that the R0 of all sensors decreases with the increase of the operating temperature. It is evident the presence of CPDs is responsible for the low resistance of CPDs/WO3. Fig. 3c and d depicts the response transient characteristics of the sensor as a function of CPDs amounts at optimal operating temperature. Compared with pure WO3, larger sensor response is observed with CPDs/WO3, and maximum response value of 14.6 is acquired by CPDs/WO3 with 3 mL CPDs to 40 ppm triethylamine. Herein, the distribution of CPDs is beneficial for the electronic and chemical sensitization of WO3 for molecular adsorption reaction with triethylamine. Under optimal CPDs amount, the sensor delivers the fastest response and recovery time (Fig. 3d). In detail, the response time is shortened from 268 to 63 s and the recovery time is shortened from 340 to 164 s (Fig. 3e), that is, the response and recovery time is improved by approximately 4 times and 2 times, respectively. Selectivity is considered as a crucial parameter for gas sensors, and we compared the response of the WO3 and CPDs/WO3 sensor toward several industrial gases, such as methanol (CH4O), ethanol (C2H6O), acetone (C3H6O), isopropanol (C3H8O), toluene (C7H8), ammonia (NH3), and triethylamine (C6H15N), as depicted in Fig. 3f. It is clear that the triethylamine leads to higher response compared to other targets, and thus the CPDs/WO3 sensor possesses excellent triethylamine selectivity. To make a feasible evaluation of the optimal work condition and facilitate the determination of the sensor advantages in different aspects, a five-axe spider-web diagram including the response (S), selectivity (Q), operating temperature (1/T) and response/recovery rate (1/τres and 1/τrec) was presented in Fig. 3g. In the spiderweb diagram, the blue area (CPDs/WO3) is obviously larger than the orange area (WO3), indicating the superiority of CPDs/WO3 sensor with balanced performance. Moreover, a summary of triethylamine sensor based on metal oxide related sensing materials is compared (Gui et al., 2019b; Li et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2016; Wei et al., 2020a; Wu et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2016, 2020a, 2020b; Yang et al., 2019; Zhai et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Zou et al., 2017), as listed in Table 1. Undoubtedly, the CPDs/WO3 sensor exhibits the most desirable sensing characteristics for triethylamine detection.

Fig. 4a depicts the transient plots of the WO3 sensor (at 170 °C) and CPDs/WO3 sensor (at 140 °C) to triethylamine with various concentrations. It can be seen that the CPDs/WO3 sensor is superior to the WO3 sensor in the measured range from 0.5 to 40 ppm. Fig. 4b shows the corresponding calibration curves, and linear relationships are demonstrated between sensor response and triethylamine concentration. The response slope in the linear regime for the WO3 and CPDs/WO3 sensor is 0.30 ppm−1 and 0.46 ppm−1, respectively. According to the least-squares method of fitting in the linear regime, the theoretical detection limit (D
L ) of gas sensor is the value of gas concentration when sensor response is three times greater than the standard deviation of noise signal (rms
noise ) (Hou et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2018), which can be derived as follow:
(1)
D
L
ppm
=
3 · rms
noise
slope
=
3
slope
·
V
x
2
N
where N is the number of data points used in the plot fitting, V
X
2 is the standard deviation of the data points used, and slope is the slope of the fitting plot of response versus triethylamine concentration. Accordingly, V
X
2 is gathered as 0.0505. Thus, the theoretical detection limit of the CPDs/WO3 sensor is calculated to be 46 ppb, which is lower than that of the WO3 sensor (110 ppb). Both the D
L of above sensors are extremely lower than the human toxicity level of triethylamine exposure (threshold limit value of 1 ppm) (Wei et al., 2020a; Zhang et al., 2020). To further explain the surface reaction, the transient response (S(t)) was fitted according to the Langmuir-Hinshelwood equation (Bai et al., 2014b):
(2)
S
t
=
S
max
( 1 −
e
− k
C
a
t
)
where S
max is the maximum response of gas sensor, k is the surface rate constant, and C
a is the gas concentration. The fitted results are depicted in Fig. 4c and summarized in Fig. 4d. With the increase of triethylamine concentration, the response time basically presents a downward trend, and the sensor response synchronously increases with the increase of surface rate constant. To be noted, the response time of the sensor to 0.5 ppm triethylamine is shorter than that to 1 ppm triethylamine, probably due to the nonuniform of gas concentration after injection at the initial stage.

Generally, the gas-sensing behavior of semiconductor gas sensors is on the basis of the adsorption and reaction of target gases on active gas-sensing materials, and the sensor response is determinate at a certain concentration of adsorbed gas molecules. That is, the sensor response reflects the concentration level of adsorbed gas molecules that take part in surface contact reactions. Thus, the first derivative of the response can reflect the concentration change of adsorbed gas molecules. Accordingly, the sensor response and its first derivative can be used to represent the kinetics of gas adsorption-desorption process. To simulate the kinetics of gas adsorption and desorption, the transient plots of the WO3 and CPDs/WO3 sensors to 40 ppm triethylamine were transformed by a derivative method (Bai et al., 2014c). As shown in Fig. 5a and b, the first derivative of transient response toward time (dS/dt) was plotted as a function of transient response (S), and the change of the dS/dt can be used to represent the adsorption-desorption kinetic during the sensing process. At the initial stage, the sharp peak of the adsorption-desorption cycle suggests fast adsorption speed of triethylamine on the sensor surface, and the adsorption plays a dominant role in surface contact reactions. As the sensing process continues, the adsorption of triethylamine slows down gradually until the adsorption and desorption turn out to be in equilibrium. For the WO3 sensor, the desorption rate of triethylamine is obviously faster than the adsorption rate (Fig. 5a). Here, the slower adsorption rate may be the reason for the lower response of the WO3 sensor. As shown in Fig. 5b, the larger area of adsorption-desorption cycle indicates that the adsorption-desorption kinetic of the CPDs/WO3 sensor is thoroughly superior (about 67 times) than that of the WO3 sensor. Moreover, the gas desorption of the CPDs/WO3 sensor is greatly improved after the introduction of the CPDs, that is the desorption and diffusion of product gases generally take faster than the diffusion and adsorption of triethylamine.

To demonstrate the influence of humidity on the CPDs/WO3 sensor, the sensor response towards 40 ppm triethylamine were measured under different relative humidity. As shown in Fig. 6a, the sensor demonstrates tolerable decrease of response in the humidity range of 11–97% RH. This is mainly attributed to the occupied active site of the CPDs/WO3 by water molecules competed with the adsorption of triethylamine. Moreover, the linear relationship indicates that the influence of humidity on the sensor can be accurately predictable. Fig. 6b depicts the long-term stability of the CPDs/WO3 sensor upon exposure to 40 ppm triethylamine at 140 °C. It can be seen that the sensor response maintains basically steady values with receivable fluctuation for 15 days, which implies that the CPDs/WO3 sensor has excellent long-term stability and reproducibility. In order to evaluate the anti-interference performance, the two types of sensors were measured under three mixed gases of ethanol, isopropanol and triethylamine (Mixed gas I: ethanol: isopropanol: triethylamine=10 ppm: 10 ppm: 10 ppm; Mixed gas II: ethanol: isopropanol: triethylamine=20 ppm: 20 ppm: 20 ppm; Mixed gas III: ethanol: isopropanol: triethylamine=40 ppm: 40 ppm: 40 ppm). As shown in Fig. 6c and d, when testing the same concentration of triethylamine in the presence of ethanol and isopropanol, the response of WO3 sensor decreases slightly while the response of CPDs/WO3 sensor does not change, suggesting the superiority of the CPDs/WO3 sensor in mixed gas detection.

After addition of the CPDs, the light absorption of CPDs/WO3 is strengthened across the visible region, and an obvious redshift is observed in the absorption edge, indicating the presence of interaction between the CPDs and WO3 (insert of Fig. 7a). The optical band gap (E
g ) of the WO3 and CPDs/WO3 were estimated from the absorbance data using the following equation:
(3)
ahv =
A ( hv −
E
g
)
m
where α, h, v is the absorption coefﬁcient, Planck constant and light frequency, respectively, and m= 0.5 for direct-band-gap semiconductor oxide (Liu et al., 2014). As shown from the Tauc plot (Fig. 7a), the band gap of WO3 and CPDs/WO3 is estimated to be 2.74 and 2.71 eV, respectively. The decreased band gap of CPDs/WO3 may be attributed to the formation of defects within the forbidden band gap of WO3 by introducing CPDs. It means that the CPDs might play a crucial role in exciting electrons to the conduction band and enhancing the sensor performance.

As is well known, photoluminescence (PL) is closely related to the intrinsic defect states in transition metal oxides (Wang et al., 2020; Zargouni et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2009). After illumination excitation, the characteristic spectrum of a certain wavelength is emitted when the defect electrons or valence band electrons of the metal oxides transfer to a higher energy level and then return. To further reveal the gas-sensing mechanism, the PL-Gaussian fitting was applied to identify the active species and defect information in the sensing samples. Generally, the fluorescence emission in the ultraviolet region (Near-Band-Edge emission, NBE) and the visible region (Deep-Level emission, DL) is caused by free-exciton and transitions of excited subcenters in the deep energy levels, respectively (Bai et al., 2014a). Generally, the higher area ratio of DL to NBE (I
DL /I
NBE ) represents the higher defect density, since the DL emission is usually related to intrinsic defects and oxygen vacancy. In Fig. 7b–d, the PL spectra were fitted into subpeaks by deconvolution. For pure CPDs sample, there is only DL emissions, which is in accordance with its super photoluminescence behavior in Fig. 1b. For pure WO3 and CPDs/WO3 sample, the relative PL ratio of I
DL /I
NBE was calculated to be approximately 4.49 and 42.48, respectively. The higher I
DL /I
NBE value of CPDs/WO3 indicates that more defects present in the CPDs/WO3 structure and provide more electrons for the redox reaction with the adsorbed gas molecules.

As illustrated in Fig. 7e, when the sensor is exposed to air atmosphere, the oxygen molecules adsorb on the surface of CPDs/WO3 and capture free electrons from the conduction band to create chemisorbed oxygen species (O2 +e− → O2 −, O−, O2 −), resulting in the formation of an electron depletion region. Upon exposure to triethylamine (TEA), the TEA molecules react with the chemisorbed oxygen species (C6H15N + O− → CO2, N2, H2O + e−), which releases the trapped electrons to the CPDs/WO3 and decreases the electron depletion layer and the height of voltage barrier. Thus, the resistance of the sensor alternately decreases in TEA and increases in air. Herein, the synergistic effect of WO3 and CPDs is believed to be substantial for the improved triethylamine-sensing properties of the CPDs/WO3 sensor. Firstly, the CPDs generated high electron conductivity (Fig. 7a) enhances the electron mobility and thus improves the interfacial charge-transfer process. Secondly, the CPDs aroused defect feature (Fig. 7d) results in an increased number of reactive sites for gas adsorption and an uplifted band bending for the electrons to pass through the electron depletion region. Additionally, the as-formed slit-like pores (Fig. 1e and Fig. S2) enables the gas molecules to diffuse and interact with the CPDs/WO3 efficiently. All these factors desirably contribute to the receptor and transducer functions, thereby achieving improved gas-sensing performance of the CPDs/WO3 sensor, including higher response, lower operating temperature, lower detection limit, and shorter response/recovery time.

In summary, we have demonstrated a facile strategy for activating nanosheet-assembled tungsten oxide microspheres with carbonized polymer dots as desirable gas-sensing layer. The adsorption-desorption kinetic reveals the gas desorption of CPDs/WO3 was greatly improved after introduction of the CPDs. The CPDs/WO3 sensor exhibits improved sensing characteristics for triethylamine detection, including high response (14.6), short response/recovery time (63/164 s), low operating temperature (140 °C) and small detection limit (46 ppb). The performance improvement is attributed to the promoted receptor function and transducer function, mainly derived from the abundant slit-like pores for gas diffuse, desirable defect feature as reactive sites, favorable 0D–2D interface for charge transfer and transport. This work not only demonstrates a novel strategy for sensitized metal oxide semiconductor gas sensors with high response and low power consumption but also looks into the fundamental understanding of carbonized polymer dots gas sensing.

Mingxin Zhang: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Visualization. Zhihui Zhao: Methodology, Validation. Bin Hui: Resources, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Jianhua Sun: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Jianxin Sun: Investigation. Weiliang Tian: Methodology, Validation. Ziqi Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Kewei Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yanzhi Xia: Methodology, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ZKT04, GZRC202007), the Dean Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology (No. 2017K005), and the Engineering Laboratory of Chemical Resources Utilization in South Xinjiang of Xinjiang Production and Construction Corps (CRUZD2003).
